headline: Big tech to accelerate AI data centers - The Ticker
text:
Major technology companies are increasing investment in artificial intelligence-focused data centers as demand for computing power continues to grow. The expansion reflects how AI is reshaping infrastructure spending across the tech sector.
Companies including Microsoft, Amazon and Google have disclosed rising capital expenditures tied to new data center projects. These facilities are designed to support AI workloads, cloud services and enterprise customers.
The scale of investment has grown alongside AI adoption across industries.
Data centers built for AI require significantly more computing power and electricity than traditional facilities, increasing demand for specialized hardware, cooling systems and energy. Investment activity has extended beyond cloud providers.
Reuters reported that it recently invested $2 billion in CoreWeave, a cloud computing firm focused on AI infrastructure, to help expand data center capacity and meet the rising demand for advanced computing.
As companies race to secure computing resources, the strain on U.S. power grids has become more visible.
The Wall Street Journal reported that the country's largest power grid operator, PJM, has warned that rapid data center growth is creating capacity challenges in several regions.
Consulting firm ICF forecasts that U.S. power demand in 2030 will be 25% higher than in 2023, driven largely by data center expansion. Electricity demand from AI facilities is growing faster than utilities were designed to handle.
Data centers often require dedicated substations and long-term power agreements, which increases pressure on local infrastructure and planning agencies.
As a result, data center location decisions are becoming more strategic. Companies are increasingly prioritizing regions with reliable power supply, available land and faster permitting processes, shifting expansion toward areas that can support large-scale infrastructure growth. 
Industry analysts have noted that delays in grid upgrades and construction timelines can slow AI deployment, raising costs for companies competing to bring new products to market.
These constraints have made infrastructure planning a central part of corporate AI strategy.
Amazon's expanding footprint illustrates the scale of these facilities. The company's largest data center campus, which contains servers, cooling systems and power equipment that are built at a massive scale to support AI training and cloud services, The New York Times reported.
Such facilities can consume as much electricity as small cities. Their growth has prompted renewed scrutiny of where data centers are located and how communities absorb the costs associated with increased energy usage.
For tech companies, these investments are viewed as necessary to remain competitive.
Access to computing capacity now plays a central role in determining how quickly firms can develop, deploy and monetize AI products.
Infrastructure costs are also becoming a larger component of AI strategy. Power availability, land access and construction timelines now influence where companies expand, adding new constraints to growth. Despite these challenges, big tech shows little sign of slowing the build-out.
Data center construction has become a critical pillar of the AI economy, tying digital innovation directly to physical resources.
As AI adoption expands across business and consumer applications, the race to build and power data centers highlights how infrastructure will play a central role in the industry's next phase of growth.

headline: Buildings and Grounds Committee approves Wise master plan, Fontaine data center
text:
The Board of Visitors Buildings and Grounds Committee convened Thursday afternoon, where members unanimously approved updates to the College at Wise's 2035 Master Plan and a schematic design for a new Research Data Center at Fontaine Research Park. The Committee also heard updates on design plans for the School of Data Science and Entrepreneurship building and revisions to the University's fiscal year 2027 Major Capital Plan.
The Buildings and Grounds Committee oversees maintenance and construction of University buildings and is responsible for matters related to the University's land use.
The Committee unanimously approved Wise's Master Plan for the College at Wise during Thursday's meeting, which outlines plans for the campus through the next nine years. Chancellor Donna Henry and University Architect Alice Raucher presented the plan and according to the presentation, Wise plans to construct additional academic buildings and complete dorm renovations to accommodate first-year housing needs with an additional 80-100 beds.
"We are a growing campus," Henry said. "Over the past three years, we have grown enrollment about 5 percent a year. Next fall, [we are] just a little bit nervous because we may outgrow our housing on campus, but it is an exciting thing that we're looking at, and we do have some solutions for housing for the fall."
Raucher added that Wise plans to develop four new academic buildings, expand and improve Greear Gymnasium and construct a multi-use facility for hosting athletic competitions, public events and conferences. The plan also proposes four new academic building sites and additional research buildings.
Raucher also presented design plans for a new research data center at Fontaine Research Park, which will be powered by an energy plant that begins with four megawatts of capacity and will have the ability to grow to 16 megawatts. U.Va. Health IT will also move to this building, Raucher said. The data center will be designed to maximize energy and site efficiencies with its close proximity to the existing energy plant at Fontaine.
"The design of the data center is meant to complement the design of the energy plant, as well as draw upon the existing materials of the adjacent buildings at Fontaine," Raucher said.
Beyond action items, the Committee also heard two presentations, including another by Raucher which discussed designs for a new School of Data Science and Entrepreneurship building to be housed on Emmett-Ivy Corridor. The building will be connected to the original Data Science building, which was completed in 2024, and will hold entrepreneurship-focused spaces, classrooms and research space.
Only two sites along the University's Ivy Road property do not yet have specific plans, Raucher said. These sites, which are adjacent to the Data Science buildings, will have landscaping and a temporary trash collection area for nearby buildings until the University identifies programs which they could house.
"With the completion of this project and then the Center for the Arts, only sites 2A and 2B remain unprogrammed, quite extraordinary for a 10 year plan so far," Raucher said.
Board member David Okonkwo raised concerns about pedestrian access to the Ivy Corridor from Central Grounds. Raucher said that the city of Charlottesville is planning to widen sidewalks along Emmett Street and to update the intersection of Emmett and Ivy to synchronize traffic light signals, both of which will increase accessibility.
Senior Vice President for Operations Lois Stanley later provided an overview on the University's FY2027 Major Capital Plan - a long-term plan which is reapproved by the Board annually and ensures that major construction, renovation or infrastructure projects align with the University's priorities - to Committee members. The proposed plan will cost $1.8 billion total, down from the FY2026 total of $2.1 billion.
The University is proposing the addition of five new projects to the plan - a multi-purpose practice field for U.Va. Athletics, two new Health System suites, road construction near North Grounds and renovations to the Health System's Core Lab. After making additional revisions, Stanley will seek approval on the plan from the Committee at its next meeting in June, she said.
The Buildings and Grounds Committee will reconvene during the next meetings of the Board June 4 and 5.

headline: Buildings and Grounds Committee approves Wise master plan, Fontaine data center
text:
The Board of Visitors Buildings and Grounds Committee convened Thursday afternoon, where members unanimously approved updates to the College at Wise's 2035 Master Plan and a schematic design for a new Research Data Center at Fontaine Research Park. The Committee also heard updates on design plans for the School of Data Science and Entrepreneurship building and revisions to the University's fiscal year 2027 Major Capital Plan.
The Buildings and Grounds Committee oversees maintenance and construction of University buildings and is responsible for matters related to the University's land use.
The Committee unanimously approved Wise's Master Plan for the College at Wise during Thursday's meeting, which outlines plans for the campus through the next nine years. Chancellor Donna Henry and University Architect Alice Raucher presented the plan and according to the presentation, Wise plans to construct additional academic buildings and complete dorm renovations to accommodate first-year housing needs with an additional 80-100 beds.
"We are a growing campus," Henry said. "Over the past three years, we have grown enrollment about 5 percent a year. Next fall, [we are] just a little bit nervous because we may outgrow our housing on campus, but it is an exciting thing that we're looking at, and we do have some solutions for housing for the fall."
Raucher added that Wise plans to develop four new academic buildings, expand and improve Greear Gymnasium and construct a multi-use facility for hosting athletic competitions, public events and conferences. The plan also proposes four new academic building sites and additional research buildings.
Raucher also presented design plans for a new research data center at Fontaine Research Park, which will be powered by an energy plant that begins with four megawatts of capacity and will have the ability to grow to 16 megawatts. U.Va. Health IT will also move to this building, Raucher said. The data center will be designed to maximize energy and site efficiencies with its close proximity to the existing energy plant at Fontaine.
"The design of the data center is meant to complement the design of the energy plant, as well as draw upon the existing materials of the adjacent buildings at Fontaine," Raucher said.
Beyond action items, the Committee also heard two presentations, including another by Raucher which discussed designs for a new School of Data Science and Entrepreneurship building to be housed on Emmett-Ivy Corridor. The building will be connected to the original Data Science building, which was completed in 2024, and will hold entrepreneurship-focused spaces, classrooms and research space.
Only two sites along the University's Ivy Road property do not yet have specific plans, Raucher said. These sites, which are adjacent to the Data Science buildings, will have landscaping and a temporary trash collection area for nearby buildings until the University identifies programs which they could house.
"With the completion of this project and then the Center for the Arts, only sites 2A and 2B remain unprogrammed, quite extraordinary for a 10 year plan so far," Raucher said.
Board member David Okonkwo raised concerns about pedestrian access to the Ivy Corridor from Central Grounds. Raucher said that the city of Charlottesville is planning to widen sidewalks along Emmett Street and to update the intersection of Emmett and Ivy to synchronize traffic light signals, both of which will increase accessibility.
Senior Vice President for Operations Lois Stanley later provided an overview on the University's FY2027 Major Capital Plan - a long-term plan which is reapproved by the Board annually and ensures that major construction, renovation or infrastructure projects align with the University's priorities - to Committee members. The proposed plan will cost $1.8 billion total, down from the FY2026 total of $2.1 billion.
The University is proposing the addition of five new projects to the plan - a multi-purpose practice field for U.Va. Athletics, two new Health System suites, road construction near North Grounds and renovations to the Health System's Core Lab. After making additional revisions, Stanley will seek approval on the plan from the Committee at its next meeting in June, she said.
The Buildings and Grounds Committee will reconvene during the next meetings of the Board June 4 and 5.

headline: BUSINESS; Using low-carbon concrete to trim data centers' climate harm
text:
Data centers siphon huge amounts of energy to power artificial intelligence. But their environmental footprint starts to balloon even before the first server switches on due to the immense amount of carbon-intensive concrete needed to build them.
As the data center buildout surges, with construction beginning on multibillion-dollar facilities from Texas to Wisconsin, tech companies are becoming buyers of low-carbon concrete.
Through 2030, data center expansion is expected to require 2 million metric tons of cement, the binding agent in concrete, according to environmental nonprofit RMI.
If these facilities are constructed with traditional concrete, they could generate 1.9 million metric tons of CO2 emissions, according to Chandler Randol, a senior associate with the group's cement and concrete team. That's the equivalent of annual emissions from 415,000 gas-powered cars.
Concrete -- as well as steel -- is a significant portion of the emissions associated with building data centers, said Katherine Vaz Gomes, a decarbonization engineer at Carbon Direct, a climate advisory firm.
"The boom in data centers is providing an opportunity to evaluate, address and move on the carbon impacts of concrete," said Vaz Gomes. "As AI infrastructure explodes, data center construction is accelerating and bringing concrete demand with it."
Microsoft Corp. announced a deal with low-carbon concrete maker Sublime Systems last year, allowing the tech company to purchase up to 622,500 metric tons of cement over a period of six to nine years.
"As demand for AI and cloud services grows, we are advancing how we design, build, and operate our datacenters and campuses. Decarbonizing the built environment is a crucial element in this process," Melanie Nakagawa, Microsoft's chief sustainability officer, said.
In August, Amazon.com Inc. struck a similar deal with startup Brimstone, but didn't disclose how much concrete it would purchase.
To help the industry, Amazon, along with Meta Platforms Inc. and Prologis Inc., signed a low-carbon concrete pact in September called the Sustainable Concrete Buyers Alliance.
The group was brought together by RMI and the Center for Green Market Activation, a nonprofit focused on decarbonizing industries. Its aim is "sending clear demand signals to producers" that they'll find buyers of their low-carbon products, said Heather House, principal of RMI's cement and concrete practice.
Amazon has started using low-carbon concrete in data centers under construction in Virginia and Oregon, and the company also invests in startups with the "potential to help accelerate decarbonization progress," according to Chris Roe, the company's director of worldwide carbon.
In addition to Brimstone, Amazon has invested in CarbonCure, another startup that helps retrofit concrete plants to create a low-carbon product. The tech company has also put in place carbon intensity standards for any concrete it uses that factor in production and transportation emissions, according to Roe.
The maturation of the green cement industry will require a lot of capital, said Christina Theodoridi, policy director for industry at the Natural Resources Defense Council. The technology for low-carbon cement is there, but the industry hasn't yet scaled to meet the demand coming over the next several years. The partnerships between tech companies and startups are crucial.
"Having an offtake agreement with a large data center is a very clear demand signal," Theodoridi said. "That's a really critical mechanism to scale up those technologies."
Funding from big tech companies might not be enough, though. The green concrete industry was set to receive roughly $1.6 billion in support from the Inflation Reduction Act. But President Trump pulled the funding last year.
"That was going to breathe real momentum into the industry," Theodoridi said. "The data centers present an opportunity for which we would have been much better prepared had we continued those investments in heavy industry."
The effects of funding cuts are starting to ripple through the industry. In December, Sublime laid off 10% of its staff, citing a loss of $87 million in government funding. It also said it was pausing construction on its factory in Holyoke, Mass., which was scheduled to open as early as this year.
Data center operations will put much more carbon in the atmosphere than their construction. Operational emissions, however, can be lowered over time as data centers become more efficient or rely more heavily on clean energy. But using green building materials is the only way to reduce what's known as the embodied emissions associated with data centers.
And for tech companies to meet their climate goals, those emissions will have to be dealt with.
"Quite a few of those large tech companies actually have quite aggressive and meaningful climate targets," said Chris Magwood, a manager on RMI's carbon-free buildings team. "Internally, they obviously have identified that data centers and particularly the concrete use in those data centers is a key driver for them."

headline: Carbondale adopts standards for data centers
text:
Carbondale preemptively adopted regulations for data centers this week, joining a growing number of Lackawanna County communities to manage the rapidly expanding industry through zoning.
Council voted 6-0 to adopt an ordinance Tuesday night amending Carbondale’s zoning to address data centers, though the city has yet to receive a proposal from a data center, Mayor Michele Bannon said Wednesday. Councilman Joseph Connor was absent.
“We’re doing it to be proactive to ensure that the city has legislation in place if there is a need for that,” Bannon said.
Carbondale is now the second Upvalley community and at least the eighth municipality in Lackawanna County to adopt zoning standards for data centers. In addition to its Upvalley neighbor in Mayfield, Carbondale joins Archbald, Blakely, Clifton Twp., Covington Twp., Dickson City and Jessup to adopt legislation that defines data centers and related uses, regulates them by establishing specific conditions necessary for their approval, and restricts where developers can construct them.
Municipalities in Pennsylvania are required to allow for every type of lawful land use somewhere within their borders, from data centers to landfills to strip clubs, and failing to do so could open towns up to legal challenges.
By adopting zoning standards before receiving any data center applications, local governments retain more control over future developments. So far, every town to approve data center zoning has designated them either as conditional uses or, in Carbondale’s case, special exceptions.
Conditional uses and special exceptions similarly establish additional data center-specific zoning standards that developers must adhere to while also requiring special approval for any project. In both cases, developers must apply with a municipality and attend a public hearing to speak about their projects, giving residents the opportunity to ask questions and testify to support or oppose an application, with the key difference being who actually approves or denies a proposal. Conditional uses go through councils or township supervisors, while zoning hearing boards grant special exceptions.
So far, only Carbondale and Dickson City have designated data centers as special exceptions, with other communities using conditional uses.
Tuesday’s meeting had a larger-than-normal turnout, Bannon said, attributing it to news of the ordinance allowing for nuclear power with special approval.
“We explained to them through that, that if they’re not going on the grid, they would be able to use the nuclear power that way,” the mayor said.
While municipalities have largely banned on-site power generation for data centers, only allowing backup generators, Carbondale and Covington Twp. provide zoning standards for small modular reactors, or SMRs, which Carbondale’s zoning defines as:
“A small nuclear fission reactor designed to be manufactured off-site and installed at a location to provide energy to buildings or commercial operations. SMR designs may include pressurized water, Generation IV, thermal-neutron, fast-neutron, molten-salt, and gas-cooled reactor models. An SMR must be fully licensed and permitted by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) and may only be considered by Special Exception approval.”
The technology is still under licensing review by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission and will likely be deployed in the late 2020s to early 2030s, according to the U.S. Department of Energy.
Carbondale officials saw that power is a significant issue with data centers, and the ordinance is “just making sure that we had our i’s dotted and t’s crossed on that,” Bannon said.
The ordinance designates data centers as special exceptions in the city’s light industrial, heavy industrial and interchange activity center zones in:
• Carbondale’s southwest corner, continuing north along Brooklyn Street.
• Between Cottage Street and Gordon Avenue.
• Around North Scott Street, Apple Avenue, Clidco Drive and Dundaff Street.
• The industrial park surrounding Enterprise Drive.
Requirements in the ordinance include providing letters from water, electric and sewer utilities confirming they can adequately serve data centers; a mandatory raw water needs analysis and water feasibility study estimating the impact on wells within 1,000 feet of property lines; conducting preliminary, interim and as-built sound studies as well as a vibration study showing no vibrations extend beyond property lines; and submitting decommissioning plans addressing e-waste and hazardous materials, among other requirements.
Carbondale officials were able to draw from the experience of other municipalities that already regulated data centers, Bannon said.
“It doesn’t matter how big the company is as long as we have a use class and we follow the rules and we do what we’re supposed to do as a municipality — to protect our residents and to have fair and equitable land uses,” she said. “That’s what we’re all about here.”

headline: Concerns heighten as data centers spread - The Advance-Titan
text:
As Wisconsin lawmakers move to regulate the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence data centers, experts warn the projects could strain water resources and energy grids in the Fox Valley.
UW-Oshkosh's Sustainability Director Brad Spanbauer said the pros don't outweigh the cons for AI data centers.
"AI data centers in Wisconsin, driven by massive AI projects, are straining regional water resources and energy grids, placing them under the scrutiny of the Great Lakes Compact," Spanbauer said. "While these 'hyperscale' projects promise economic growth, they require massive amounts of water for cooling and energy, raising concerns about potential violations of the Compact's strict water-diversion rules, which restrict taking water outside the Great Lakes Basin, and highlighting a regulatory gap regarding high-volume water use."
According to the Wisconsin Examiner, the Wisconsin State Assembly passed Assembly Bill 840 by a 53-44 vote on Jan. 20 which allows the regulation of AI data centers being built in Wisconsin. 
Spanbauer said due to high water and energy consumption, energy bills for Wisconsin households may increase. 
"The Wisconsin grid was already stressed to meet demand," Spanbauer said. "Adding more fossil fuel-powered infrastructure to supply energy to the data centers is only going to exacerbate growing problems." 
Andrew Kell, Renew Energy's policy director, said Wisconsin is producing 17.4 gigawatts of energy and a large-scale data center uses around four gigawatts of energy. 
"The electricity usage over the course of a year, what they're projecting, it's like the city of Milwaukee," Kell said. 
A doctoral student at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, Gabriel Shapiro, said energy usage is directly linked to water usage. 
"It turns out that 90% of the data center's water use comes indirectly from the electricity that it requires, and that by putting in these closed loop systems, it cancels out those benefits, because they're actually using way more electricity," Shapiro said. "These closed loop systems have to be kept at high pressure all the time to be able to keep the closed loop so it's directly connected."
Shapiro said there are benefits from data centers shutting down operations for a few hours out of the year to pull down the strain on the energy grid.
"Gas plants don't have to get built to be able to meet the demand if they can just agree to be for a small portion of the year," Shapiro said. 
Kell said Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers has a goal to get zero carbon emissions by the year 2050. 
"It's going to take a lot of investments," Kell said. "It's going to take a lot of policies and economic transition to make that happen." 
Shapiro said there is creative thinking when it comes to adding more ways to get towards this goal by 2050.
"There's ways to mix farming with solar, grazing sheep underneath the solar panels," Shapiro said. "There is degraded land that is not being farmed, where the solar should go. There's toxic waste sites, landfills that can be covered and highway medians." 
Shapiro said the Fox Valley can take action by crafting safe guards surrounding the data center boom.
"The Fox Valley could center itself as being a leader and saying, 'we want to say that we're going to do it our way,'" Shapiro said. "Essentially, not letting these big tech companies kind of trample in and set up on their own terms, but instead, here's a set of requirements."

headline: Council hears presentation on possible data center
text:
Editor’s Note: Over the coming weeks, we will be taking a deep dive into data centers, their impact, benefits and what having one in Washington could mean for residents should one be built here.
At Monday’s Washington City Council meeting, representatives from Outrigger Industrial gave a presentation about a possible data center that could be built near I-69.
The city has been asked to sign a willing to serve letter that states the city could meet the water and sewer needs of the facility. The potential site for the facility whose end-user is unknown at this time, already has access to adequate power since sites must be near the transmission lines. Still, signing that letter does not mean a data center is coming to Daviess County.
Outrigger has until April 20 to get additional information to the building commissioner’s office and its plan will need to be heard before the city plan commission as well.
According to the Environmental Resilience Institute at Indiana University, data centers contain hundreds of server racks with computer processors, data storage and networking cables. The facilities can be very large, some up to 500,000 square feet. Those centers can house cloud storage, deliver digital services like video streaming or e-commerce.
Indiana already has 89 data centers across the state with over half being around Indianapolis according to datacentermap.com.
The centers have both local and state requirements they must make. Zoning is handled at the local level and can also put other requirements such as noise level limits, set back requirements and more, in place. There are also state approvals including permits from the Indiana Department of Environmental Management that may be needed for water use and discharge, emergency generators and fuel storage tanks. There are also air permits due to the use of diesel backup generators and cooling equipment as well as wastewater permits due to water usage for equipment cooling and water withdrawal, stormwater discharge or wastewater as well as other general permits.
Technology for data centers is constantly evolving in efforts to lower energy and water footprints for the facilities. On-site solar panels, battery storage and geothermal are being tested to power operations and closed-loop water cooling and air-side economizers help to recycle water and reduce energy demands.
More information on data centers can be found at mostpolicyinitiatives.org, datacentermaps.com, greatlakes.org, hecweb.org, epa.gov and betterdatacenterproject.com.

headline: County groups kill moratorium, approve recommendation for data center near Waverly
text:
Feb. 20—A project that could create central Illinois' first data center near Waverly was the subject of contentious back-to-back meetings in Sangamon County.
Sangamon County Board's zoning and land use committee shot down a six-month moratorium on any vote to approve the zoning or construction of a data center during its Thursday meeting in Springfield. The county's Zoning Board of Appeals then unanimously approved a non-binding recommendation to the board for data company CyrusOne to receive conditional permitted use to build a $500 million data center in Talkington Township, a few miles east of Waverly.
The data center project has courted controversy since it was first made public last year. Local residents have expressed concerns about the potential consequences it could have on farmland, fauna and electricity rates, among other things. Even the state has seemingly cooled its jets on data centers; Gov. JB Pritzker proposed a two-year suspension on tax credits for data center construction during Wednesday's State of the State address.
"With the shifting energy landscape, it is imperative that our growth does not undermine affordability and stability for our families," Pritzker said.
RELATED: Proposed data center near Waverly draws heavy criticism at board meeting
The zoning and land use committee met first, voting 5-3 against the moratorium prior to the public comment section. Many who spoke to the committee during the section expressed disappointment that they were not able to raise their concerns before it voted. Much of the criticism echoed what had previously been said about the project.
Auburn resident Alexa Martinie said she was worried about the potential impacts the data center could have on the health and happiness of her community.
"My neighbor earns $750 a month on Social Security and she earns $120 a month on food stamp benefits," Martinie said. "My energy bills have doubled since I moved to Auburn and that is before this data center has gotten here. Where is my neighbor expected to go if she is priced out of living in Auburn, a town she has lived in for her entire life?"
Toward the end of the meeting, several spectators shouted for another vote on the moratorium and shamed members of the committee.
Several representatives for CyrusOne delved into more of the specifics during the Zoning Board of Appeals meeting, which took place immediately after the zoning and land use committee. The project, which would be built on 280 acres of farmland adjacent to the Double Black Diamond solar panel farm, would consist of four main buildings and take an estimated five to seven years to construct, according to director of location and power strategy Bradd Hout.
CyrusOne also sought to address areas of concern, including water usage, noise pollution and electricity usage. Representatives said that, if built, the data center would use non-evaporative cooling, which uses less water than other methods of cooling data centers, and the facility would have barriers in place to minimize noise. Rural Electric Convenience Cooperative President Sean Middleton said there is enough power available on the current grid to support the project.
After the presentation, several audience members voiced their concerns about the project to the board and challenged CyrusOne's assertions about the project, occasionally arguing with board members. Several people from Waverly, including farmer Treg Caruthers, made an appearance.
Caruthers, whose family raises livestock, noted that he and several of his family members live less than two miles from the proposed site, which is "a closer than maybe most people think." He also said that the county should be mindful of what the project could do to Lake Springfield, where the city of Springfield sources its water.
"I know you've got containment and everything else set up, but accidents happen," he said. "2.8 million gallons of diesel fuel, I would be a little bit worried."
Sangamon County Board's next meeting — the soonest it could make a final vote on the project — is scheduled for 6 p.m. March 23 at the Bank of Springfield Center.

headline: Data center developer interest in Taneytown could spark new regulations
text:
After a "very preliminary" inquiry from a data center developer, Taneytown Mayor Chris Miller said the city is looking into legislation that would establish guidelines for how potential data centers could operate.
While neighboring Frederick and Baltimore counties have seen a surge in data center interest in recent months, Carroll is not home to any data center projects. Taneytown has not yet received an official permit application or site plan from any developer.
However, Taneytown doesn't have any local laws that would govern where and how data centers could be built, which Miller said would "leave the door open" for a developer to build however they want. At the city Planning and Zoning Commission's meeting last Monday, commission members offered thoughts on how they would want to regulate data centers, and Miller said the city is starting to draft new laws to ensure that any data centers that do come in are "airtight."
"We want to make sure any legislation we put in place is in the best interest of the residents in terms of utilities, energy and water," Miller said.
Taneytown's ability to supply water and energy for a data center was a top concern at the planning and zoning commission meeting, where commission members had mixed reactions to the concept of data centers in the city.
"The big thing is going to be utility draw, both water and power," said City Council member Harry Meade. "All jokes aside, our grid is fragile sometimes, with transformers and things of that nature, so that would dictate location."
According to Carroll County's Water Resources Element, a report examining water supply in each of the county's municipalities, Taneytown does not have enough water supply to meet potential demand if the city's current land area were to be fully built out. The city could increase its water system capacity through system improvements. Taneytown draws all of its drinking water from the Triassic rock aquifer.
Meade said that the city's industrial district has stronger electrical infrastructure and might be able to better support a large project like a data center.
Andrew Gray, Taneytown's director of planning and zoning, said his office had reviewed a report by the American Planning Association detailing how data centers can sometimes be located in smaller office or retail buildings, rather than the "giant buildings in the hinterlands" that might come to mind for such projects.
City Manager James Wieprecht suggested that the commission look into a law that would allow data centers only on industrial land.
"Since our code is currently silent on that use, we would like to be able to address it so we have an idea how we want to handle it if we do get an application for one," Wieprecht said. "Do we want to limit them to the restricted industrial district, for example? Do we want them to have different requirements, because of the use, than other industrial uses?"
Data center discussions in Taneytown are "preliminary, preliminary, preliminary," Gray said. The city is planning to have more in-depth discussions about data center legislation and may take action in the coming months, Miller said.

headline: Data center development could stall under new Illinois move
text:
Data-center developers might soon have another reason to be wary of Illinois.
Gov. JB Pritzker wants to suspend tax incentives for data centers in hopes of reducing demand for electricity as rates soar.
As he seeks a third term and with an eye on a possible White House run, Pritzker is focused on affordability, a mantra for Democrats nationwide seeking a populist message.
"We need to think critically about our future energy usage with the needs of Illinois households at the forefront," Pritzker said last month as he laid out a $56 billion spending plan for the fiscal year that begins July 1. "With the shifting energy landscape, it is imperative that our growth does not undermine affordability and stability for our families."
Widespread use of artificial intelligence platforms such as ChatGPT has fueled an unprecedented wave in construction of massive data centers that use 1 gigawatt or more, or enough to power nearly 1 million homes. But as electricity rates have soared, so has public opposition to data centers across the Midwest, including Illinois. Projects in Naperville and Yorkville have been rejected recently.
Many of these data center mega-projects have bypassed Illinois for Indiana and Wisconsin in large part because of a state law known as the Biometric Information Privacy Act. Losing state incentives adds to the challenge, industry insiders say.
"The proposed pause on Illinois' Data Center Investment Program could further discourage investment at a time when the industry is facing significant regulatory challenges and uncertainty in Illinois," said Brad Tietz, director of state policy for the Data Center Coalition, a trade group.
"Suspending this critical economic-development tool would put the state at a strategic disadvantage in attracting data center projects that are bringing significant benefits to neighboring states and helping make life more affordable for their residents."
The policy illustrates the political tightrope Pritzker is walking as he chases re-election as governor and potentially higher office as a billionaire in the Democratic Party. Before he was elected, Pritzker was a venture capitalist focused on technology. He made quantum computing a focus of his economic-development strategy.
Data centers, or buildings that house massive numbers of computer servers, don't provide a lot of long-term full-time jobs. But data centers provide a lot of construction work at a time when the pace of other commercial development, such as office buildings, has not rebounded from the COVID-19 pandemic.
Labor groups, which praised Pritzker's plan to jumpstart the development of new nuclear plants to combat rising prices, are critical of his data-center decision.
"A moratorium on tax incentives for data centers would not stop development - it would simply send projects, jobs and billions in private capital to neighboring states," said Climate Jobs Illinois, a group that includes multiple unions.
"Developers have choices, and if Illinois becomes less competitive, they will choose to build elsewhere - while Illinois ratepayers continue to shoulder the cost of exporting power without capturing the local jobs, tax base growth, property tax relief and other significant economic benefits."
Clean-energy groups, however, praised Pritzker's plan.
"As data centers continue to consume massive amounts of electricity and water while bringing pollution to our doorsteps, it is critical we reassess this industry, its impacts on Illinois, and the taxpayer handouts offered to them," the Illinois Clean Jobs Coalition said in a statement.
Jack Lavin, CEO of the Chicago Chamber of Commerce, praised Pritzker for keeping spending in check in his new budget and focusing on fiscal stability. But, he said: "We are concerned about the proposal to pause the data-center tax credit program, which will eliminate a proven economic-development tool."
Illinois is one of more than 30 states that offer tax credits and other incentives to data centers, mostly sales tax exemptions on the millions of dollars in servers and other equipment, as well as construction materials. Some states also offer property-tax incentives, which are highly prized by developers.
From the start of the program in mid-2019 through the end of 2024, Illinois provided $983 million on 27 projects with a combined investment of $8.1 billion and 591 new jobs. Seven more deals were approved last year and three more have been approved this year, according to the Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity.
Pritzker's proposal to suspend the program isn't as big an impediment as the biometric privacy law, but it could make things worse.
"In a place where we've got BIPA hanging over our heads, it sends the wrong message about the state," says Todd Bateman, founder of Intelligence and Strategic Advisors, an Oak Brook real estate adviser focused on data centers. "There are a lot of people who have drawn a red line through Illinois. If you cut the tax abatement, and I wasn't going there before, I'm definitely not going there now. Two problems don't make a solution."

headline: Data center discussed at Greensburg City Council meeting
text:
Near the end of Tuesday evening’s relatively short Greensburg City Council meeting, Councilor Jamie Cain read an email aloud he had sent to his fellow Councilors so they “all have the same information.”
Cain referenced a meeting in late 2024 when the Council approved an application for tax abatement on 340 acres of land annexed by the City of Greensburg as an economic development area. It was noted the Indianapolis-based company Jumping Bison LLC intended to construct a multi-building data center on the annexed site, but no groundbreaking had occurred.
More than 40 Decatur County residents attended Tuesday’s meeting, taking all the available seating and even standing in the hall. They were present because they have concerns about that multi-building data center.
“I wanted to take a moment and acknowledge that we have all received a few emails regarding the annexation that we implemented in 2024 and the information surrounding that,” Cain said.
“As you all recall, we were petitioned by individual local landowners to annex their property through a super voluntary annexation, meaning no parcel was forcibly annexed by the city. This annexation was for an area in and adjoining the Business Enterprise Park, an area marketed for over 15 years by the Greensburg, Decatur County Economic Development Corporation as an area for industrial growth.
“The request was to approve an industrial zoning, in agreement with that use and both the city and Decatur County comprehensive plans, which showed community growth in that area.
“Then we approved an Economic Development Agreement granting partial 10-year tax abatement for the real property. This incentive tool and scoring process are ones we have used for years with countless other local and national businesses.
“We are in a position to supply the water. Our facility has 6.7 million gallons per day treatment capacity, and we currently treat 2.5 million gallons per day. This new industrial user would account for less than 3% of our treatment capacity and rank them number five on our largest customer list behind Honda, Decatur County Rural Water, KB Foods, Delta Faucet and just ahead of the Greensburg Community Schools.
“It is also worth remembering that the company is covering all infrastructure costs; no infrastructure improvements or installations are being paid for by the City of Greensburg Utilities.
“All storm water or runoff will be through a storm water management system as outlined in our unified development ordinance and storm water ordinance. These systems reduce uncontrolled runoff when managed properly.
“While there have been many concerns about electricity, the City of Greensburg has no oversight or control over electric utilities or influence over the policies they implement. The electricity for the area is served by Hoosier Energy, not the City of Greensburg or even Duke Energy, which serves most of the city.
“As a status update for council members, there are no pending decisions before the city, on any boards or commissions. As I am aware, there is no timeline for any progress related to the potential project.”
The proposed data center was not on the agenda and Cain’s presentation was for informational purposes only. The council took no action regarding the data center and did not address it or any concerns that have been expressed about it.
Greensburg City Council meetings are held at 6:30 p.m. the second Tuesday of the month in the large meeting room at Greensburg City Hall, 315. W. Washington Street. All meetings are open to the public unless otherwise noted.

headline: Data center measure advances
text:
WA lawmakers aim to regulate facilities, protect power grid
With data centers expected to become the largest source of electricity demand in the Pacific Northwest, Washington legislators are pressing ahead with a bill aimed at protecting the grid and offsetting potential hikes for utility ratepayers.
House Bill 2515 passed out of the Appropriations Committee on Monday afternoon, with two Democrats and all of the Republicans on the panel opposed. Reps. April Berg, D-Mill Creek, and Larry Springer, D-Kirkland, cast the two Democratic "no" votes. The bill next awaits a floor vote in the House and still needs Senate approval.
"These policies seek to protect ratepayers by ensuring new data centers are picking up the whole tab for new growth," said Rep. Beth Doglio, D-Olympia, who is the bill's lead sponsor and chair of the House Environment and Energy Committee. She also said the bill would help grid reliability and improve transparency on water and energy usage.
Before the Appropriations Committee passed the bill, it made two significant changes. Lawmakers adopted a Republican-backed amendment, which eliminated an annual state fee for data center operators. And they loosened clean energy requirements for the facilities that were in the original bill draft.
Money from the eliminated fee would have gone to help low-income families weatherize their homes and to support higher education programs.
Data center backers say those moves are a step in the right direction, but that the bill will still stifle innovation and hurt the economy.
Washington is already among the top 10 states with the highest number of data centers. That's partially thanks to tax incentives for the centers that the Legislature approved in recent years.
The centers provide the computing power for vast amounts of internet-connected technology that people have come to rely on. And they are expected to grow rapidly to support artificial intelligence.
What changed?
As written, the bill requires utilities to establish a tariff or policy for data centers by 2027, to ensure the centers cover the costs utilities face in supplying them with enough power. The idea is that those added costs aren't passed on to other ratepayers.
The legislation also calls for data centers to curtail electricity use - possibly turning on generator power - when the grid is strained, and to take other steps to manage the demand they put on the grid.
Additionally, data center operators would be required to share a sustainability report, information on server cooling technology and data on water and energy use and emissions.
Opponents argue that, despite the bill targeting "emerging large energy use facilities," it unfairly singles out data centers. Some manufacturing facilities like semiconductor plants and food and beverage processors use just as much electricity, said Dan Diorio, vice president of state policy for the Data Center Coalition.
Before the latest changes, the bill required data centers to also pay the state an annual fee based on energy usage, levied at half a penny per kilowatt hour. Projected to bring in over $30 million a year, that fee was cut following passage of an amendment from Rep. Mary Dye, R-Pomeroy, the top Republican on the Environment and Energy Committee.
"We know that data centers really improve the economic conditions of communities that really have not had much opportunity," Dye told the Standard. "By putting a tax on data centers, you make our state unattractive for that opportunity."
Supporters of data centers specifically highlight local tax revenue and jobs they've created in more rural parts of the state, in places such as Quincy.
The bill also originally mandated data centers switch to using emissions-free energy by 2035, but that date moved to 2045 under changes backed by Doglio. This change aligns with the state's timeline in existing law to eliminate greenhouse gas emissions from the grid.
Diorio said the timeline puts everyone - data centers, utilities and other large electricity users - on the same page, but that the bill still falls short.
An average household has 21 Wi-Fi-connected devices, according to the Data Center Coalition.
Those devices are backed by cloud services, one of the primary functions of a data center, and "the backbone of the internet and modern economy," Diorio said. When the grid is strained, "data centers can't just turn the internet off," he added.
The Data Center Coalition also contends that cooling systems for the facilities are trade secrets that companies shouldn't have to disclose.
"The type of cooling technology you're using is very much a competitive edge because it allows you to be more efficient," Diorio said.
Complex issue
Puget Sound Energy, Washington's largest investor-owned utility, is already racing to find enough renewable energy to meet state mandates. Last week, Amazon outbid PSE for a large solar energy farm in Oregon. While the utility supports the intent of the data center legislation, it finds the bill does not address challenges related to this increased competition, said Andrew Padula, a spokesperson for Puget Sound Energy.
"We have been attempting to help policymakers understand the considerable complexity of this issue," Padula added.
Berg, one of the two Democrats who opposed the bill in the Appropriations Committee vote, said tax incentive legislation for data centers that she sponsored in 2022 also established the state's first labor and environment standards for facilities.
"I think we absolutely need to keep having conversations that protect ratepayers, that protect our climate and that protect our communities," Berg said in an interview. "I really want to sit down and be collaborative because I do know of communities like in Quincy where their property taxes have been cut in half."
Berg also sponsored an amendment to remove the fee that data center operators would pay to the state, but withdrew it, clearing the way for Dye's identical amendment to pass. The following fields overflowed: REFERENCE = 02-12 A1 WSS-Legislature-data centers_A01

headline: Data center tax break exposes flawed incentive strategy
text:
The Ohio Tax Credit Authority's recent decision to award a $4.5 million tax credit to a data center project backed by The Carlyle Group should prompt a broader consideration of just how Ohio defines meaningful "economic development."
When generous public resources are deployed for projects that yield only marginal public benefit, it's not just a questionable investment - it's a misalignment of priorities.
The Tax Credit Authority generally does good work and is, on balance, a plus for the state. But it flopped with aiding a $136 million expansion of Ark Data Centers facilities in Akron and Independence.
The numbers, on their face, are stark: $4.5 million in tax incentives in exchange for just 10 new jobs. It's not a cash subsidy; the tax break is a 50% sales tax exemption on data center equipment over 10 years. Even so, the subsidy amounts to $450,000 per job - a figure that raises eyebrows in any context, let alone when the recipient is a global private equity firm managing hundreds of billions in assets.
As U.S. Sen. Bernie Moreno, a Republican, put it in a March 16 letter to Carlyle Group criticizing the deal, "That's not economic development; that's a sweetheart deal for Wall Street while everyday Ohioans are forced to foot the bill." (The letter was first reported by Axios.)
It's difficult to argue otherwise. The promise of job creation has long been the central justification for public subsidies. But when the return is so relatively meager, the rationale collapses.
This is not an argument against business investment in Ohio. Companies choosing to expand in the state should be welcomed. And like it or not, public assistance, in the form of tax credits, is a valuable tool as Ohio competes with other states for projects.
Indeed, Matt Dolan, the chief executive officer of economic development organization Team NEO, told Cleveland.com that the tax credit was vital to Ark Data Centers choosing Ohio for the expansion, and that without it, the company would have moved its existing facilities - it has two in Akron, and one each in Independence and Canton - out of the state, too. And this project, like all that receive state tax credits, does support spending that helps other businesses.
But there is a difference between fostering a competitive business climate and writing checks to corporations that have little (or no) real need for public assistance.
The Carlyle Group, with about $477 billion in assets under management, is not a startup struggling to get off the ground. It is precisely the kind of entity that should be able to finance its own expansion without tapping into taxpayer funds.
Moreno's critique also underscores a deeper issue: the opportunity cost.
The $4.5 million allocated to this project could have been invested in schools, or infrastructure, or targeted workforce development programs that yield broader and more lasting benefits. Public dollars are finite, and every subsidy carries a tradeoff. In this case, the tradeoff appears lopsided.
The concerns extend beyond job creation. Data centers, while often touted as engines of the digital economy, present unique challenges that complicate their value proposition. Chief among them is energy consumption. These facilities - currently numbering about 200 in Ohio - require enormous amounts of electricity, placing additional strain on local power grids. Without proper safeguards, those costs often are passed along to ratepayers - meaning households and small businesses effectively subsidize not only the tax break, but also the infrastructure needed to support the project.
Here again, Moreno's warning is worth heeding. He points to the importance of the "Ratepayer Protection Pledge," an initiative announced March 4 by President Donald Trump that calls for companies to bear the full cost of their energy use and related infrastructure. The idea is simple: if a project is profitable, it should stand on its own merits, not rely on hidden subsidies embedded in utility bills. (An Ark Data Centers spokesperson told Axios that the company's Ohio facilities are not "hyperscale operators" like Meta or Google, and that, therefore, a call to sign Trump's ratepayer protection pledge is not applicable.)
This episode raises a fundamental question: Shouldn't Ohio demand more?
If the state is going to offer incentives, it should insist on robust job creation targets and guarantees that infrastructure costs will not be shifted onto the public. Instead, this deal appears to offer benefits to the company, with limited upside to the public.
The broader pattern is troubling. Across the country, states are engaged in a race to attract data centers, often competing to offer the most lucrative incentives. But this competition risks becoming a race to the bottom, where public resources are deployed with diminishing returns. Ohio should not follow that path.
Moreno's call for The Carlyle Group to voluntarily forgo the subsidy may be aspirational, but it highlights an essential point: This deal is not inevitable. It is the result of policy choices - choices that can and should be reconsidered.
Economic development should be about building a stronger, more inclusive economy. That means prioritizing investments that create meaningful employment, support communities and deliver a clear return on public dollars. A $4.5 million subsidy for 10 jobs fails that test.
Ohio taxpayers deserve better than symbolic job creation and opaque cost-shifting. They deserve policies that reflect both fiscal responsibility and a genuine commitment to shared prosperity. Until that standard is met, deals like this one will continue to erode public trust.

headline: Data center tax breaks are on the chopping block in some states
text:
Feb. 24—After years of states pushing legislation to accelerate the development of data centers and the electric grid to support them, some legislators want to limit or repeal state and local incentives that paved their way.
President Donald Trump also has changed his tone. Last year he issued an executive order and other federal initiatives meant to support accelerated data center development. Then last month, he cited rising electricity bills in saying technology companies that build data centers must "pay their own way," in a post on Truth Social.
As the momentum shifts, lawmakers in several states have introduced or passed legislation that aims to rein in data center development by repealing tax exemptions, adding conditions to certain incentives or placing moratoriums on data center projects. Virginia lawmakers, for example, are considering ending a data center tax break that costs the state about $1.6 billion a year.
"Who is actually benefiting from these massive data centers that, in many cases, are the size of one or two shopping malls combined?" asked Michigan Democratic state Rep. Erin Byrnes, who introduced a proposal to repeal the state's data center tax exemptions. "They have a large footprint in terms of land and energy usage. And by and large, it's not going to be the average resident who lives near a data center who's going to benefit."
Over the past few years, more data centers have been built in an effort to meet the demand for digital processing power, which has rapidly increased as more artificial intelligence systems come online. Data centers house thousands of servers that are responsible for storing and transmitting data required for internet services to work.
But as local communities voice growing outrage over rising electricity prices and environmental concerns brought by data centers, such as water and energy use, lawmakers in several states are hoping to slow data center development. By limiting incentives or placing moratoriums on new projects, state legislators are hoping to give themselves more time to determine whether the massive facilities are worth losing millions or more in tax revenue each year.
Some experts also say that developers and tech companies have exaggerated some of the benefits they bring to local communities. While the promise of new jobs sounds attractive, local leaders may face other concerns, such as the effects of diverting construction resources away from other purposes and higher energy costs caused by AI, said Michael Hicks, an economics professor at Ball State University in Indiana.
"A lot of households — and the people that are elected by households — and local governments are becoming more unnerved by the public pushback to data centers," Hicks said.
Tech developers and data center operators are concerned, however, that the changes could hurt the rapidly growing industry. And most states and localities already require developers using incentives to follow certain requirements, said Dan Diorio, the vice president of state policy for the Data Center Coalition, a lobbying group for the data center industry.
State lawmakers have to consider how changes to incentive programs could upend years of construction, which has long-term business impacts, Diorio said.
"I think data centers are very much the backbone of the 21st-century economy," he said. "We're generating economic activity in states, contributing to state-level GDP, contributing significantly to labor income and state and local tax revenue, and creating significant amounts of jobs. I mean, we're just jumping into something preemptively here."
Incentives granted
At least 37 states offer incentives that are available to data centers, including sales tax exemptions and property tax abatements, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. Sales tax exemptions, the most common incentive, allow data center developers to buy computers and other equipment at a much lower cost.
"I think these are one of many factors that the data centers are looking at, along with the cost of electricity, the cost of construction, land and things like that," said Nicholas Miller, a policy associate at NCSL. "These incentives are one way that states are trying to pitch themselves as competitive to this industry."
In 2020, Maryland implemented a program that exempts data centers from sales and use taxes if they provide at least five jobs within three years of applying to the program and invest at least $2 million in data center personal property. The first four years of the program cost the state $22 million — but $11 million of that came in 2024 alone, as the costs grew, Democratic state Del. Julie Palakovich Carr said.
Concerned about this and the impact of data centers on residents' electricity bills, Palakovich Carr introduced legislation this year that would repeal the state's sales and use tax exemptions for personal property used at data centers. The measure, which is under consideration in the House, would also restrict localities in the state from eliminating or reducing assessments for personal property used in data centers, which drew opposition from the Maryland Association of Counties.
The amount of money states are forfeiting to provide tax breaks for data centers is increasingly concerning, Palakovich Carr said.
"Unfortunately, that's the turn we're seeing across many other states," she said. "The price starts out maybe in line with what we think it's going to be. But over time it just costs more and more."
Similar bills that would repeal or halt state incentives for data centers have been filed in Arizona and Georgia.
"When we look at potential subsidies for businesses, I'm really looking at it from a frame of incentivizing new behavior rather than just giving away money for things that the companies were going to already do anyways," Palakovich Carr said. "I think it's really important that once these things get put in place, we look at the data and see what's happening on the ground."
In 2024, Michigan enacted sales and use tax exemptions on certain data centers through at least 2050.
Now, with developers looking at more than a dozen sites for potential data centers, public sentiment has soured, said Byrnes, who had voted against the measure. Communities across the state began organizing in an effort to stop data centers from coming to their neighborhoods because of environmental concerns and energy costs, she said.
The outcry prompted Byrnes to co-sponsor a bipartisan package of three bills that would repeal the 2024 law.
"We're taking a stand with this legislation to say that we don't believe data centers should be offered these exemptions," she said. "I believe it aligns with public sentiment."
Lawmakers in a handful of states — including New York, Oklahoma and Vermont — have filed bills that would place a temporary moratorium on all data center projects and require studies of their impacts.
Georgia Democratic state Rep. Ruwa Romman introduced a measure this session that would put a moratorium on new data center projects until March 2027. The proposal would give the legislature time to study the impact of data centers on the state's natural resources, environment and other areas.
"We have such a beautiful state and it would be a damn shame to completely and utterly wreck it and its landscape for short-term gain," Romman said. "These data centers aren't bringing jobs. They're saying they're bringing the revenue, but there's a ton of fine print on the revenue that's coming in. So, I've been urging my colleagues from every side of the political spectrum to just take a beat."
In 2021, the Oklahoma legislature approved a measure from current Republican House Speaker Kyle Hilbert that excludes new data centers from qualifying for an exemption program that allows certain manufacturers not to pay property taxes for their first five years in business. Any data centers that qualified for the program in the five years prior to the law, however, can continue to apply for exemptions.
This year, as more project proposals were made, Hilbert introduced legislation to ensure no data centers could "slip through the cracks."
"These aren't the days of being able to build a data center, cut deals with NDAs, then start turning dirt before the constituents even know what's happened," Hilbert said. "Those days are over, and data centers need to be proactive in their messaging and talking to people about their concerns."
Costs vs. benefits
Last year, Virginia, home to the most data centers in the country, gave up $1.6 billion in sales and use tax revenues from data centers, state data shows. That's a 118% increase from the previous year, according to a report from Good Jobs First, a watchdog group that focuses on economic development incentives. Another report from the group said Georgia is expected to lose at least $2.5 billion to data center sales tax exemptions this year, 664% higher than the state's previous estimate.
Virginia state lawmakers are considering legislation that would require data centers to achieve high energy efficiency standards and decrease their use of diesel backup generators in order to be eligible for the state's sales and use tax exemption. The measure, which passed the House, is now moving through the Senate.
Before the end of his term, former Virginia Gov. Glenn Youngkin, a Republican, suggested a provision in his proposed state budget that would extend the data center tax incentive from 2035 to 2050. The Senate's budget bill, however, would end the incentive altogether on Jan. 1, 2027. It's not clear if state leaders, including current Democratic Gov. Abigail Spanberger, support the measure.
While states can put a specific number on the tax losses, it's much more difficult to determine how much data centers contribute to local communities and the state, Miller said.
Virginia brings in a significant amount of revenue from the property taxes for each facility. Local construction firms, restaurants and other small businesses also benefit from ongoing projects, he said.
"This is the big question," Miller said. "With all economic development projects, it's generally a lot easier to measure the cost of the incentive directly versus the benefits."
The changing incentive landscape may cause instability within the data center industry, said Diorio, of the Data Center Coalition. Data center projects are large-scale capital investments that play out for several years, but changing policies could upend that progress.
"When states look at these policies or consider abrupt ends to programs, that creates significant market uncertainty," Diorio said. "It will have a significant long-term impact on the viability of that market for data center development. Industries are very responsive to market signals, and any kind of uncertainty will bring up a red flag because you're looking to invest for the long haul."

headline: Data center's owner revealed
text:
DULUTH - Google expects to open a second large data center in Minnesota, this one in Hermantown, the northeast Minnesota town where residents have pushed back on both the project and the secrecy that's enshrouded it for months.
The technology giant announced March 3 that it plans to build a data center in a rural corner of Hermantown under an agreement with Minnesota Power. It will also work with Twin Cities-based developer Mortenson on its proposed 1.8 million-square-foot campus.
The city of more than 10,000 "is a natural fit for this project," with a climate that supports air cooling as opposed to water, along with a "resilient power grid and a dedicated and motivated workforce," Kate Franko, Google's regional head of public affairs for data centers, said in a statement.
Concerns for the environment and potential use of Lake Superior's coveted stores of water have been central to controversy about the data center, to be built on more than 200 acres in the city next to Duluth.
Last week, Google announced a data center in Pine Island, Minn., near Rochester. It spent a year lobbying behind the scenes to influence state tax breaks and data center regulations. There are at least a dozen large-scale data centers proposed in Minnesota.
Google said Tuesday it would support the development of clean energy resources in the region with 300 megawatts of wind energy and 400 megawatts of batteries. That is a massive amount of new battery power for Minnesota Power, which has no significant energy storage on its system but hopes to expand that infrastructure to supply electricity when the wind is not blowing and the sun is not shining.
Google also said it would work with Minnesota Power to reduce grid strain and give $5 million to the utility for efficiency and affordability programs for its low-income customers.
Hermantown resident Katie Hawkins is part of a local grassroots opposition group that has decried the lack of transparency surrounding the data center project. She said $5 million is "a slap in the face" considering the company's resources.
Last year, state lawmakers passed legislation for what is a new industry here. Data center companies have to comply with guidelines aimed at protecting the state's climate goals, water and household electric bills, and pay for conservation projects that help low-income families. But they also get to keep some tax breaks that they say are critical to data center development.
Google will pay all costs to connect to the grid and its share of system costs, said Jennifer Cady, vice president of public policy and external affairs for Allete, Minnesota Power's parent company.
"Existing customers will not be negatively impacted," she said in an interview.
Data center supporters say they are crucial for job creation and tax base growth. Companies behind them say they are critical infrastructure for the cloud computing and artificial intelligence needs of a modern economy.
Opponents worry about noise and light pollution, traffic and impacts to land and water and energy consumption.
The campus is expected to use 50,000 gallons of water daily, equivalent to the daily use of about 160 homes.
Four 300,000-square-foot buildings would house tech infrastructure; also on the site would be two office buildings, a warehouse, equipment yards, parking for nearly 600 and utility pads, according to an application to the city.
While large data centers demand massive amounts of energy, neither Google nor Minnesota Power has said how much the Hermantown complex would need to operate.
Julie Pierce, Minnesota Power's vice president of strategy and planning, said the utility didn't have an exact number yet, and Google hasn't notified the utility about additional electricity needs for an air-cooled system.
It is also unclear how much Google's investment in Hermantown could be.
The build is expected to happen in phases over eight to 10 years.
Hermantown and Pine Island are two of at least eight Minnesota cities that signed nondisclosure agreements related to a data center project.
Public records show Hermantown has known since September 2024 that a data center was proposed. The city wouldn't disclose the nature of the project, dubbed Project Loon, until it was revealed last fall in city emails obtained by the Minnesota Star Tribune.
Residents mobilized quickly, protesting the data center in droves at public meetings. Many complained about government secrecy, rate hikes and devalued properties, as well as potential long-term harms to the region's environment and a forever-changed community footprint.
Acknowledging worries, Cady said Minnesota's regulated utility structure is different from some other states', where price spikes and supply-and-demand problems have arisen with data centers.
"I think Minnesota has all the right pieces in place to protect customers," she said.
Hermantown City Administrator John Mulder said in a statement that while there will be more public process steps ahead, Google's "commitment to meeting Minnesota's strong environmental standards, their desire to invest in local infrastructure in ways that do not raise rates for our residents and businesses, and their efforts within the local labor market will be incredible."
The Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy has sued several Minnesota cities, including Hermantown and Pine Island, over claims of inadequate environmental reviews. The nonprofit's strategy officer, Aaron Klemz, said Tuesday that Google announcing both of its data center projects in quick succession shows how much has happened "behind the scenes."
Google, which pulled out of a data center project in Becker several years ago, did not respond to an interview request.
The agreement between Google and Minnesota Power will be reviewed by the state's Public Utilities Commission later this month.
Walker Orenstein of the Minnesota Star Tribune contributed to this story.
janah@startribune.com

headline: Data center's use would power 150,000 homes; Alliant Energy reveals more details about Meta's Beaver Dam data center
text:
BEAVER DAM DATA CENTER | ELECTRICITY
State regulators ordered Madison-based utility Alliant Energy to release more details about the $1 billion data center project in Beaver Dam. 
Unlike We Energies, which wants to create ?a separate electric rate for hyperscale data centers, Alliant has proposed an individual contract rate agreement with Facebook parent Meta to supply power for the company's 700,000-square-foot data center campus in Beaver Dam. 
But the plan that the utility filed with regulators redacted a significant amount of information, including energy usage and ratepayer protection provisions. 
At a hearing Feb. 26, Public Service Commission officials told Alliant to refile the ?contract rate agreement with fewer redactions, adding that not doing so could flout state open records ?law. 
"We do want to keep our records open for public inspection," said Administrative Law Judge Michael Newmark. "It seemed like the redactions were not going to allow us to do the basic functions of open governance in that sense." 
Attorneys for Alliant countered that the PSC had previously approved the redacted version but ?said they would work with Meta to refile. The utility resubmitted its application on Feb. 27. 
"Alliant Energy met the request of the PSCW and we await their review of our application," the utility told the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel in a statement. 
Environmental and consumer advocate groups have raised concerns about the lack of transparency in Alliant's case. Nonprofit law firm Midwest Environmental Advocates sued the PSC ?in December for the release of projected electrical loads for Meta's Beaver Dam data center as well as Vantage's $15 billion campus in Port Washington. 
The lawsuit, filed in Dane County Circuit Court, is ongoing. 
The PSC has extended ?the public comment period on Alliant's application through March 9. The commission has until late March to determine whether the utility must remove more redactions. 
Here's what the new ?filing revealed: 
How much energy will the data center use? 
Alliant will supply up to 220 megawatts to the project, according the application. That's enough to power more than 150,000 homes, but significantly less than projected loads for the two major data center projects in We ?Energies' service territory. 
Vantage's Port Washington campus is estimated to need 3.5 gigawatts ?at full buildout. Microsoft's two data centers ?under construction in Mount Pleasant will use around 900 megawatts, and the tech giant has plans to build 15 more on the site. 
Meta's Beaver Dam project is the first large-scale data center proposed within Alliant's service territory. It is currently under construction and plans to come online in 2027. 
What are the ?terms of the contract? 
Under the current proposal, Meta would enter ?a 10-year service agreement with the option for two five-year extensions. The company would pay a fee to cover system costs if it uses less energy than anticipated. 
The contract also includes termination charges should Meta end the service agreement early, which Alliant says will protect ratepayers from shouldering the infrastructure costs. 
"If you use energy, you pay ?for it," Alliant said. "Customers who use large amounts of energy pay for the electric grid investments needed to serve them, not ?existing customers." 
Attracting large data centers benefits all ratepayers by spreading fixed costs across a larger customer base, Alliant said in its application. 
In public filings submitted on Feb. 10, PSC staff raised concerns that Alliant's service agreement is shorter than similar contracts in other states. For example, a data center customer in Michigan has a minimum contract term of 15 years on top of a ?five-year ramp-up period. 
"The contract term provided by the applicant is shorter and potentially more limited in providing cost recovery protections ?for other customers," PSC analyst Tyler Meulemans said in filings submitted Feb. 10. 
'A good step forward' for transparency 
The PSC's move is "a good step forward" for transparency, said Tom Content, executive director of Wisconsin's Citizens Utility Board, an intervenor in the case. 
In public filings, the board echoed Muelemans' concern about ?the length of the service agreement, suggesting it be extended to 15 to 20 years. It also called for stronger early ?termination fees. 
The Citizens Utility Board also asked the ?PSC to require Alliant create a separate rate category even if the individual contract agreement is approved. Content said the move would create consistency across Alliant's service territory, much like We Energies' plan in southeast Wisconsin. 
"The same rules of the road are going to be applied to Microsoft, to Vantage in Port Washington and any other data centers in that part of the state," Content said. 
The PSC ?is expected to rule on the Alliant and We Energies cases later this spring.
