headline: User Name: =
text:
The Blueprint
 Developers must prioritize power availability and grid capacity before site selection
 Community concerns over noise, water use and environmental impact are rising
 “Bring your own power” solutions add cost and regulatory complexity
 Transparency and community engagement are critical to project approval
Last month, the Eagan City Council voted unanimously to halt development for one year of new data centers within 500 feet of existing homes. It may be the first Minnesota city to impose a moratorium on data center development, according to MPR News.
Eagan already has two data centers, including a 155,400 square-foot facility on the former Thomson Reuters campus that Ryan Cos. plans to renovate up to modern standards. But residents pushed back on the idea of approving new computing facilities that they said would make a racket and consume excessive amounts of water and energy. City officials agreed, saying they needed more time to study a phenomenon Minnesota municipalities have relatively little experience with.
Though Eagan’s moratorium remains an outlier, opposition to large-scale computing facilities has increased recently. Only 37% of respondents offered unqualified support for data center development near their homes in a January poll by POLITICO. Fifty-six percent were neutral or opposed. Meanwhile, developers delayed or downsized about 36% of the data center capacity expected to come online last year, according to Sightline Climate. Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz signed a law last year setting new guardrails for data centers’ energy and water use, but some opponents said the measure doesn’t go far enough.
Robert Montejo, a real estate lawyer with Duane Morris who represents data center and energy clients, said data center developers may not fully appreciate the sensitivity of the moment.
“I truly believe we’re at a tipping pointwithout a shift in the narrative, they risk being the ‘big oil’ of the digital age,” he said in an interview.
Shifting that narrative, Montejo said, requires a proactive approach to development that balances discretion and transparency. Successful data center projects tend to position themselves as long-term infrastructure assets that benefit entire communities even regions rather than just the parties to the transaction itself, he added.
Power can make or break a data center project
Eagan’s moratorium applies to data centers larger than 20 megawatts in close proximity to residential areas. The size limit, at least, would rule out projects like the $800 million, 715,000-square-foot facility Meta is building in Rosemount or the two Google is proposing near Pine Island and Hermantown.
Google has not said how much power its proposed data centers would draw, but the size of each site and volume of offsetting energy the company is procuring suggests each will be on the order of hundreds of megawatts at full buildout. Meta’s Rosemount facility is rated at 308 megawatts, according to Cleanview, an energy data provider.
Industry insiders like to express data centers’ size in megawatt terms, rather than square footage, because they consume immense amounts of power. Meta Rosemount’s peak consumption will be nearly 2% of Minnesota’s total electric generating capacity, for example.
Those power requirements mean grid capacity is typically the first priority for data center developers. If the transmission lines running near a site don’t have sufficient headroom and can’t be upgraded cost-effectively, the site may not work out, said Will Mulhern, director of clean electricity for Fresh Energy, a St. Paul nonprofit.
“They are picking spots on some level based on that,” he said.
Interconnection headways vary greatly by location and the amount of power the customer wants to draw, but for large data centers, they’re typically measured in years. In some parts of the country, the wait stretches to seven years. That’s leading many developers to build ad hoc power plants alongside data centers.
Shorthanded in the industry as “bring your own power,” this more capital-intensive approach is worthwhile for big tech companies looking to scale AI “compute” faster. It’s not without drawbacks, however.
Colossus, a massive “AI factory” built near Memphis by Elon Musk’s xAI, for years flouted federal air quality regulations by running portable gas generators without the required permits. The facility is broadly opposed by the area’s working-class residents, who are already exposed to above-average levels of air pollution, and seen as a black mark by many in the tech industry. Earlier this month, Mississippi regulators cleared xAI to build a similar facility to power an expansion of Colossus on their side of the border.
Cleanview is tracking more than 50 gigawatts of “bring your own power” projects across the United States, around three times the total capacity of all the power plants in Minnesota. Many rely on natural gas generators, which are noisy, exacerbate local air pollution and contribute to climate change.
Such projects may be a harder sell in Minnesota. Though “behind-the-meter” power plants facilities built to serve a single customer and without connection to the public grid are not subject to Minnesota’s mandate for 100% carbon-free electricity by 2040, the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission still has the authority to approve or deny them, according to Fresh Energy. One of the factors in Amazon’s decision to abandon a data center project in Becker last year was the commission’s insistence on scrutinizing its plan to add 250 diesel-fueled backup generators at the site, Mulhern said.
“We want to see these powered with clean electricity we are very firm that any data centers that are built are not going to compromise Minnesota’s compliance” with the 2040 law, he said.
Data centers generally run round the clock, so most have backup generators to get them through the occasional grid outage. The Google and Meta facilities almost certainly will. But they will draw most of their power from the utility grid, which in Minnesota is increasingly clean. Google, at least, will also pay for more than 2 gigawatts of carbon-free power wind, solar and batteries to offset its two proposed facilities’ energy use.
By covering the cost of a significant expansion of Minnesota’s power grid, Google will ensure its presence on the grid doesn’t jack up other customers’ utility bills. That’s another requirement of the Minnesota data center law passed last year. Its clean energy plans may also help it win favor with erstwhile opponents, who have been particularly vocal in Hermantown.
“It’s always positive to hear that clean energy is being discussed for a proposal,” the Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy, a leading project opponent, said in an otherwise skeptical statement reported by the regional Minnesota Public Radio affiliates.
Montejo said data center developers should get out of the tech-industry mindset that got them this far and adopt a posture closer to that of the utilities whose territories they’re reshaping.
“You don’t move fast and break things when you’re building infrastructure,” he said. “It is a slow and deliberate process [but] a better place to be overall in this discussion. ”
Being a good (and honest) neighbor
Montejo said data center developers should clearly communicate the project’s benefits, beyond new and paid-for energy infrastructure, while being honest about how they might change host communities.
For example, while large-scale data centers don’t create lots of permanent jobs, data center developers can emphasize the long-term boost to the local tax base, a key reason many communities court them in the first place. They can also commit to delivering specific “community benefits,” such as partnering with local colleges and vocational schools to scale up training for electricians, HVAC technicians and other roles critical to building and maintaining computing hubs, Montejo said.
Water use is another flashpoint for data center projects seeking local approvals, including Google’s Hermantown proposal. Many “legacy” data centers cool themselves with evaporative chillers that consume vast amounts of water.
Sometimes, local jurisdictions with excess water welcome a whale-sized new customer. But those looking to conserve water may be reassured by developers planning to use cutting-edge data center designs whose cooling systems combine closed water loops and non-water refrigerants, greatly reducing their impact on aquifers, Montejo said.
Finally, without jeopardizing sensitive negotiations, Montejo said data center developers and host communities should mind residents’ desire for information about what’s likely to be the single biggest private investment they’ve ever seen.
While non-disclosure agreements are necessary early in the process as developers engage with landowners, utilities, permitting authorities and other critical stakeholders, using them longer than necessary could invite blowback, Montejo said. The Hermantown project was shrouded in secrecy for months before land-use applications made it clear that a very large industrial facility was on the way. That was still many months before Google was revealed as the user, however.
Once it’s no longer possible to conceal the scope of a project, it may be better to go public and present a case, Montejo said.
“Education is going to be the best tool in the toolbox for developers [you don’t want] the worst-case scenario to be the only thing that’s talked about,” Montejo said.

headline: Georgians shouldn’t have pay for data center energy
text:
The Jan. 26 AJC article, “Data center tax breaks, energy costs emerge as targets for Georgia lawmakers,” highlights the Georgia General Assembly’s efforts to address data centers.
In December, the Public Service Commission certified Georgia Power’s plan to build new infrastructure, mostly gas-fired power plants, to meet expected demand from new data centers. But Georgia Power’s staff acknowledged that almost half of this data center growth may not materialize — and if or when the data centers don’t show up, Georgia residents will be left holding the bag.
The Georgia House recently passed House Bill 1063, which aims to protect residential and small-business electricity customers from bearing the costs of data centers. But this legislation only codifies existing PSC rules, omitting the most important part of the conversation: What happens if Georgia Power builds extensive new capacity for data centers, but they don’t show up?
A stronger bill in the Georgia Senate — SB 34 — offers robust consumer protections by prohibiting the shifting of costs of fuel generation and transmission for data centers onto households and small businesses.
This legislation is needed to protect Georgia Power customers, and we urge lawmakers to support it.
AIDYN LEVIN, GEORGIA PIRG ASSOCIATE

headline: Google data center coming to Pine Island
text:
[box type = "shadow"]
The Blueprint
 Google will anchor a 250,000-square-foot data center developed by Ryan Cos. on an 88-acre site in Pine Island.
 Project expected to create 500 construction jobs and 100 permanent operational positions.
 Development projected to generate tens of millions in property tax revenue and $20 million in infrastructure upgrades.
 Xcel Energy signed an agreement to supply power; project will use advanced air-cooling technology.
[/box]
Technology giant Google is headquartered in California, but it will soon have a big presence in the small Minnesota town of Pine Island, as well.
The city announced Tuesday that Google is on board as the anchor tenant in a planned data center, which will occupy part of a 482-acre site framed by 490th Street to the north, Highway 52 to the west and south, and 195th Avenue to the east in Pine Island.
Developed by Ryan Cos. US Inc., the project will feature a 250,000-square-foot data center, a 35,000-square-foot office building, greenspace and 18 acres of stormwater management on an 88-acre portion of the development site.
Ryan is also preparing and marketing “additional pad site opportunities to industrial and technology companies,” said Peter Fitzgerald, Ryan’s vice president of Real Estate development.
“We hope this will help incentivize Google to expand at this site and within our state,” Fitzgerald said.
Minneapolis-based Ryan Cos. said it hopes to begin construction later this year, adding that the project will put about 500 skilled tradespeople to work and create 100 onsite operational jobs.
While data center proponents argue that such projects create quality jobs and promote economic growth, others have raised concerns about impacts on water resources and the electrical grid, among other drawbacks.
The city approved land use applications in January. During the entitlement process, the city heard from both supporters and critics of the project, said Elizabeth Howard, Pine Island’s city administrator.
“We've really been trying to weigh those concerns on either side of the table as we've been going through the land use applications, and so we've been trying to be mindful of our residents, while trying to also bring new growth to Pine Island,” Howard said.
The city said in a press release that the project will generate “tens of millions of dollars in new property tax revenue,” along with “high-paying jobs,” and about $20 million worth of infrastructure upgrades funded by Google and the developer.
Google will establish a $25 million fund for Pine Island Public Schools “spanning the next 20 years,” the city said.
Also on Tuesday, Xcel Energy said it recently signed an agreement to supply power for the Google data center in Pine Island, which will use advanced air-cooling technology instead of water for cooling purposes.
Google will pay “all costs for its new service in line with its typical practices and Minnesota’s regulatory and legislative requirements for large loads,” according to Xcel.
An Electric Service Agreement will be filed for review with the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission “in the coming weeks,” Xcel said, adding that the MPUC must “formally approve the agreement between Xcel Energy and Google to supply power to the site. ”
Xcel Energy CEO Bob Frenzel said in a webinar last month that data center development is having a net positive impact on local communities as measured by job creation,economic developmentand even the cost of electricity.
“And I’d argue that data centers are good for economic development. They’re very expensive. They pay property taxes. They have employment. The construction jobs are good when they show up and build them,” Frenzel said.
Fitzgerald said Google will offer the “level of care and collaboration with the city of Pine Island and the state of Minnesota that Ryan expects” on its projects.
“Google’s decision to select this location highlights their trust in Ryan and desire to invest in Pine Island and Minnesota long into the future,” he added.
The Pine Island project comes at a busy time for data center proposals in Minnesota.
Most notably, Meta is building an $800 million, 715,000-square-foot data center in Rosemount. Other projects are under consideration in cities such as Apple Valley, Rosemount, Monticello, Faribault, and Hermantown, as well as Pine Island.
At the same time, the city of Eagan recently approved a one-year moratorium on certain data center projects, and a group of Farmington residents are suingto stop construction of a large hyperscale data center campus.
Overall, Minnesota builders are hopeful about the market segment.
Asked in a recent Associated General Contractors of Minnesota survey how they believe certain segments will perform in 2026, contractors were most optimistic about technology/data centers.
In its 2026 U.S. Real Estate Market Outlook, CBRE said Minneapolis is “emerging as a significant data center hub in the Midwest, evolving from a market with smaller colocation facilities to one attracting major players for large-scale developments. ”
Copyright © 2026 BridgeTower Media. All Rights Reserved.

headline: Grant County residents advocate against potential data center
text:
BY KONRAD STRZALKA
konrad.strzalka@thmedia.com
CASSVILLE TOWNSHIP, Wis. - Months after local leaders revealed a developer was exploring building a data center in Grant County, a large contingent of residents is vocally opposing such a project.
An upcoming informational event in Potosi is expected to draw hundreds of concerned residents, according to organizers.
Meanwhile, leaders in Cassville Township are considering adopting village powers giving them input over their own zoning and thus making them able to potentially block construction of a data center.
Cassville Township consists of rural areas in west-central Grant County and surrounds but is a separate entity from the village of Cassville.
Data centers house computer servers to store information for cloud-based software and to support the expansion of artificial intelligence. When local leaders in December first said a developer was interested in bringing a center to Grant County that would be similar in size to one planned in Beaver Dam - a "mega-site" valued at about $1 billion - the location was undisclosed. The Cassville area was cited as a potential site, largely due to its proximity to the Cardinal-Hickory Creek transmission line.
Grant County Economic Development Corp. Executive Director Ron Brisbois said he has heard little recently from the developer, who would build a center to sell to a technology company. The identity of the developer has not been disclosed.
"It's been very quiet," Brisbois said.
Brisbois confirmed that at one point, the developer met with him and Cassville Township leaders and was considering a location near the transmission line.
However, Brisbois said the developer reached out to him last month and indicated he was evaluating two sites outside of Grant County. Brisbois expects confirmation of whether the developer wants to proceed with a Grant County project around May.
Brisbois previously told the Telegraph Herald that if such a project comes to fruition locally, it could bring dozens of jobs to the area. The developer has taken steps to address concerns about electricity and water usage, he said.
A special meeting of the Cassville Town Board is scheduled for 6p.m. Thursday, March 12. Board members will potentially take the first steps in the process of becoming a zoned township, which would give them, rather than the county, some say over a potential data center. Cassville town board members did not respond to phone calls from the Telegraph Herald this week.
Township resident Heidi Hochhausen said she plans on attending the meeting.
"I've cleared my schedule so I can be there," she said. "I feel like it's important to be there."
Data center projects across Wisconsin and in other parts of the country have sparked concerns over their high energy consumption. State legislators have proposed differing solutions to these issues.
A September analysis from environmental advocacy group Clean Wisconsin found that two data centers approved for construction in Ozaukee and Racine counties would consume enough energy to power 4.3 million homes.
The data centers' usage of water for cooling also sparked concerns. A single large-scale facility can consume up to 5 million gallons of drinking water daily, according to Harvard Science Review. However, the Ozaukee County data center, valued at $15 billion, is expected to consume under 22,000 gallons of water daily, according to its developers.
In learning such information, Hochhausen said she worries about the natural springs near her rural property.
"I'm worried our water's going to dry up," she said. "Water's essential to life."
Kelly Simonini, who lives in the village of Cassville, said she worries a nearby data center would impact not only the environment, but the serenity of the area.
"We do a lot of late-night driving, early-morning driving, taking photos," she said. "There's so many places they can put these aside from our rural settings."
The issue of data centers has prompted chatter in all corners of Grant County. A Facebook group, "No Data Centers in the Driftless," now has 1,500 members.
County residents such as Pete Moris hope an upcoming community discussion will raise further awareness on the issue.
Moris, of Mt. Hope, is among the organizers of "Data Centers in the Driftless: A Community Conversation," which will be held at 2p.m. Sunday, March 8, at Potosi's Holiday Gardens Event Center, 101 Brewery Hollow Road. It is billed as a nonpartisan and informational event. Doors open at 1p.m.
"I don't care who you vote for, what party you're with, (protecting) the Driftless Region is something we can all agree on," Moris said. "It's truly a unique place."
Melodie Betts, owner of The Lemon Door cafe in Fennimore, also is helping to organize the event.
"When I first found out about a (potential) data center in the Driftless, I became vocal because that's what it takes," she said.
Headlining the event is Prescott Balch, a former tech executive who recently fought against a proposed data center in his Racine County hometown of Caledonia.
Moris also said Charlie Berens, who has amassed a large internet following for his Midwest-themed sketch comedy videos, will be in attendance. In the past year, Berens has taken to advocating against data centers in Wisconsin, both in his content and in public appearances.
The event also will include a Q&A.
"People are starved for information in our area," Moris said. "This is an opportunity to let folks ask questions and make up their mind."
Betts and Moris said they expect hundreds of attendees at the event.
"Based upon the reaction we've gotten and the reach on social media, we're hoping we have a big enough venue," Moris said.

headline: Green groups sue PSC over data center expansion
text:
Environmental and faith groups have escalated their fight against Georgia Power’s data center-driven expansion, asking a court to review whether regulators broke state law by approving the utility’s plan to add a slew of new power resources.
In the petition, the groups argue Georgia Power never proved all of the new power resources were needed, a requirement under state law for the Public Service Commission to approve them. They argue overbuilding for data centers that have not signed contracts with Georgia Power exposes other ratepayers to the prospect of higher bills.
The challenge lodged Wednesday in Fulton County Superior Court was filed by the Southern Environmental Law Center and the Sierra Club. The SELC is representing Georgia Interfaith Power & Light, Park Avenue Baptist Church and the Unitarian Universalist Church of Savannah. The Sierra Club is representing itself in the case, along with the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy and Sierra Club member Adrien Webber.
Their petition names the PSC as the respondent. The commission voted in December to “certify” the company’s construction of eight new gas-burning power plants, plus battery storage systems and some solar. Regulators also gave the utility permission to buy electricity from several existing power plants.
‘Unnecessary costs’
At the time of the unanimous vote to approve, Republicans held all five PSC seats.
Since then, two of the PSC’s GOP members were replaced by Democrats, who rode a wave of voter discontent over their high Georgia Power bills to claim seats on the commission. The expansion that’s being contested is the largest one in Georgia Power’s history.
The PSC’s staff estimated the company will need to collect between $50 billion and $60 billion from customers in the coming decades to pay for the expansion. Certification paves the way for the utility to charge customers for costs down the line, but Georgia Power has repeatedly vowed residential customers won’t subsidize the data center build-out.
Webber, a plaintiff in the case, said the PSC’s decision worsens fossil fuel dependence and pollution while adding “unnecessary costs that show up directly on the bills of Georgians.” The majority of the power resources Georgia Power plans to add rely on oil and gas. Burning fossil fuels is the main driver of human-caused climate change.
“We are tired of a Public Service Commission bending and outright breaking its rules and the law to give Georgia Power whatever it wants, no matter the cost to our air and water or the impact on our monthly energy bills,” Webber added.
Tom Krause, the PSC’s public information officer, declined to comment on the petition, citing the commission’s policy not to weigh in on active litigation.
In a statement, Georgia Power spokesperson Matthew Kent said the company is reviewing the petition, but said it does not believe this “positive, constructive plan for Georgia electric customers needs to be reconsidered.”
“The generation resources were properly approved and certified by the Commission. This suit has no merit and is a simple attempt to create economic and regulatory chaos,” Georgia Power said after it had additional time to review the suit.
Kent also pointed to the expansion’s purported benefits.
With the help of extra revenue from data centers, Georgia Power has agreed to put $8.50 in “downward pressure” on the average residential customer’s monthly bills for the years 2029 through 2031. But that doesn’t mean bills will drop — just that they will be $8.50 lower than they otherwise would be.
The SELC, the Sierra Club and their clients already asked the PSC earlier this year to reconsider its approval of the new power plants, but the Republican majority that still controls the commission rejected their request last month.
The petitioners have asked the court to reverse the PSC’s order and require that it reevaluate the evidence presented in the case.
The groups also asked the court to order the commission to decertify at least 757 megawatts of generation capacity, which they argue haven’t been proven necessary. That’s roughly the output of the new gas-burning unit Georgia Power is building at Plant McIntosh outside Savannah. Experts have testified it may be the most expensive gas unit proposed anywhere in the U.S.
It was not immediately clear when the Fulton County Superior Court will decide the case.
Data center, fuel cost uncertainty
If the commission’s vote stands, it will mean Georgia Power has permission to add almost 10,000 megawatts of generating capacity to its grid. That’s the equivalent of adding about nine of the 1,100-megawatt nuclear reactors at Plant Vogtle near Augusta.
The expansion is set to unfold over just five years and is overwhelmingly to generate electricity for data centers. Metro Atlanta remains one of the country’s top destinations for the computer server-filled warehouses, which are used to train artificial intelligence and drive the digital economy.
But a lot has changed since December, when the PSC approved the company’s plans.
The U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, throwing a wrench into global energy supply chains and markets. It’s unclear how long the conflict will last, but some analysts have warned prolonged disruption could weigh down the data center boom, even in the U.S.
This coverage is supported by a partnership with Green South Foundation and Journalism Funding Partners. You can learn more and support our climate reporting by donating at AJC.com/donate/climate.

headline: Harlingen calls for public hearings on proposed data center moratorium
text:
Apr. 2—HARLINGEN — Amid citizens' concerns, city commissioners are calling for public hearings, planning to impose a 120-day moratorium on data center projects to study their impact on residents, resources and the environment.
In a meeting, commissioners held off on considering a proposed ordinance aimed at protecting the city's water supply months after the Harlingen WaterWorks System's board of directors entered into an agreement that could supply effluent water to a proposed 18,000-acre data center project near Valley International Airport on Cameron County land.
"For the sake of the public, rest assured that your city commission is working hard, making sure that the steps that we take moving forward are responsible steps," Mayor Norma Sepulveda told an audience packing City Hall during a meeting Wednesday.
To call a moratorium aimed at development, the law requires officials hold public hearings, Commissioner Daniel Lopez said Thursday, adding the city's giving residents a 30-day notice of its plans to impose a moratorium.
"The public is invited to come to a commission meeting to share their thoughts on the ordinance," he said, urging residents to read the document on the city's website. "I look forward to taking up the issue so the commission and the public can learn more about the impacts data centers have on communities' infrastructure, economy and most importantly natural resources."
Now, officials are planning to set two public hearings before considering imposing a moratorium.
"During the moratorium period, the city commission, in coordination with the planning department and the city attorney, shall undertake a comprehensive evaluation of the potential impacts associated with high-intensity data processing facilities within the city of Harlingen," a resolution states.
"This review shall include a detailed assessment of the potential demands such facilities may place on municipal infrastructure, including the city's potable water supply, wastewater collection and treatment systems, electrical service coordination with regional utility providers and other critical public infrastructure necessary to support municipal services and long-term community needs."
As part of the study, officials are planning to work with WaterWorks to evaluate water supply impacts and long-term water availability while conducting an analysis of wastewater capacity and treatment impacts and determining electrical load demands and infrastructure requirements.
The study will also include an evaluation of land-use compatibility and development standards along with proposed zoning requirements aimed at regulating siting for any data center projects.
During the meeting, commissioners held off on considering a proposed ordinance aimed at protecting the city's water supply while planning to discuss the proposal with WaterWorks officials.
The proposed ordinance would call for "a policy requiring city commission approval for certain extraordinary water supply commitments outside the city limits in order to protect municipal water resources, infrastructure capacity and long-term growth planning, including legislative findings, applicability provisions, preservation of the operational authority of the Harlingen Waterworks and Sewer System, protective application and severability."
Last September, as part of a proposed project of Eneus Energy, the WaterWorks board entered into an agreement with RGV Property Group LLC in Overland Park, Kansas, potentially supplying as much as 4.6 million gallons of sewer water a day to run a data center, records show.
As part of the agreement, the company would pay WaterWorks 75 cents per 1,000 gallons of effluent water, and $1.2 million a year if it averages more than 4.6 million gallons per day at the end of a year, Roy Rodriguez, the agency's interim general manager, said, adding the company has not contacted him since entering into the agreement.
Now, Sepulveda, Lopez and City Attorney Mark Sossi are working on a proposed ordinance aimed at regulating data centers.
Last year, Eneus Energy proposed development of a 1,785-acre data center project on county land just east of the airport.
Earlier this week, Cameron County Judge Eddie Treviño Jr. said the company hasn't presented officials with a "formal" proposal.
"Cameron County has not been presented with a formal data center proposal, whether in the form of a tax abatement request, building permit application or any other official submission," he said in a message. "We've received only informal, preliminary outreach and nothing in the last several months."
"It's also important to note and clarify that Cameron County has not actively recruited nor courted data center development," Treviño said, pointing out the 1-785-acre site sits on private land.
During Wednesday's meeting, residents called on commissioners to impose a moratorium on data center projects.
"We need a moratorium, a pause, time for studies to be conducted by independent agencies — infrastructure, vibration, water, air quality and power studies," resident DawnRae Leonard told commissioners during the meeting's public comment period.
"What is the public cost analysis?" she asked. "If these data centers are so great, why are they shrouded in secrecy? You know you can't explain the wonders of data centers because you can't sell it. What happens when our water, electricity bills double? What happens about our home appraisals?"
From the podium, Jonathan Jones called on commissioners to carry residents' concerns to Treviño and the county commissioners court.
"I hope you lean on the county commission and the county judge and urge them to resist data center proposals and the customary offers of tax incentives and abatements," he said. "I know the state of Texas seems to deprive municipalities and counties of much needed power in some cases, but I think we can still fight the good fight."
Meanwhile, Sydney Rivera, who described herself as an environmental scientist, warned data center development would drain the Rio Grande Valley's water supply amid the drought.
"Future measures to build water-cooling-dependent data centers in the Valley would be disastrous," she said, adding Texas data centers used 49 billion gallons of water last year.

headline: Harlingen mayor raises questions after data center meeting
text:
Harlingen Mayor Norma Sepulveda did not get all the answers she wanted after meeting with Eneus Energy, a company in the early stages of a data center project just outside city limits, according to a statement Thursday.
Eneus Energy, a UK based company, plans to locate a data center on a 1,785-acre site northeast of the Valley International Airport, the Valley Morning Star has previously reported.
“Eneus has not purchased the property,” she said on Facebook. “There is a contract, but it is contingent upon them being able to secure what they need to move forward.”
The project falls under the jurisdiction of the Cameron County Commissioners’ Court. County officials have been pushing to withhold information requested under the Texas Information Act because it could affect “proprietary interests” of the data center company.
Norma Sepulveda
Sepulveda, who has been posting regular updates on data center developments to social media, said she is working with the city attorney on a local ordinance and potential moratorium.
A moratorium could temporarily suspend activity if approved by the city.
“If something affects our regional water supply, our utilities, or surrounding infrastructure, it matters to Harlingen whether it sits just outside city limits or not,” Sepulveda wrote.
Community members have expressed concerns about high water and energy usage if a data center were to be built.
Valley International Airport’s welcome sign is pictured Monday, Jan. 23, 2023, in Harlingen, (Rick Kelley/Valley Morning Star)
The Harlingen WaterWorks system previously agreed to provide more than 4.6 million gallons of sewer water to help run the data center.
“I will also be reviewing any potential agreement involving Harlingen WaterWorks System,” said Sepulveda, adding that WaterWorks operates with its own board, but Harlingen owns the utility.
Other data center companies have been interested in building in Harlingen, she continued, though only Eneus Energy has reached out to the city and its Economic Development Corporation about potential partnerships.
The South Texas Environmental Justice Network also obtained email exchanges between Eneus Energy and Cameron County officials regarding the data center late last month. They revealed that talks have been ongoing since early December.
Harlingen City Hall is seen in this undated file photo. (Valley Morning Star file photo)
“I have been listening and I hear that the community wants oversight from our Commission,” said Sepulveda, noting she does not take her responsibilities lightly.
Eneus Energy will conduct community engagement when they move into that phase of planning, she said, adding that she plans to visit other communities with operational data centers.
“I want to see firsthand what worked, what did not, and what they wish they had required from the beginning,” she said.
While she does not currently see a clear benefit to the city of Harlingen, the conversation is not over.
“It means the burden is on the developer to demonstrate value and protection,” Sepulveda said.
Cameron County asks Texas AG to withhold data center info
The post Harlingen mayor raises questions after data center meeting appeared first on MyRGV.com.
